actions.description: Define service objects in the `app/services` directory to encapsulate business logic.

actions.description: Implement JSON serializers in the `app/serializers` directory to format API responses.

actions.description: Define authorization policies in `app/policies` to control user access to resources.

actions.description: Isolate and encapsulate complex ActiveRecord queries in `app/queries` for better reuse and testability.

actions.name: Define custom data types and coercion logic in the `app/type
actions.description: Define custom data types and coercion logic in the `app/types` directory to extend or override ActiveModel type behavior.

actions.description: Use `rails generate` to create new models, controllers, and migrations.

actions.description: Use `rails db:migrate` to apply database migrations.

actions.description: Use `rails server` to start the development server.

actions.description: Use `rails test` to run the test suite.

actions.description: Use `rails assets:precompile` to compile assets for production.

rules.description: Create custom validators in `app/validators` to enforce specialized validation logic.

directives.description: Follow the RuboCop Style Guide and use tools like `rubocop`, `standardrb`, or `rufo` for consistent formatting.
Use snake_case for variables/methods and CamelCase for classes/modules.
Keep methods short and focused; use early returns, guard clauses, and private methods to reduce complexity.
Favor meaningful names over short or generic ones.
Comment only when necessary — avoid explaining the obvious.
Apply the Single Responsibility Principle to classes, methods, and modules.
Prefer composition over inheritance; extract reusable logic into modules or services.
Keep controllers thin — move business logic into models, services, or command/query objects.
Apply the “fat model, skinny controller” pattern thoughtfully and with clean abstractions.
Extract business logic into service objects for reusability and testability.
